questions.question: Explain the five essential components of a data communication system with an example.
keyPoints: 1. Sender: Device that transmits data (computer, phone)
2. Receiver: Device that receives data
3. Message: The actual data being transmitted
4. Transmission Medium: Path for data (cable, wireless)
5. Protocol: Rules for communication (TCP/IP, HTTP)
Example: Email sent from laptop to server

questions.question: Differentiate between simplex, half-duplex, and full-duplex transmission modes with examples.
keyPoints: Simplex: One-way communication (TV, keyboard)
Half-duplex: Two-way but not simultaneous (walkie-talkie)
Full-duplex: Simultaneous two-way (telephone, video call)
Key difference: direction and timing capability

questions.question: Define attenuation and explain how it affects signal transmission. Mention methods to overcome attenuation.
keyPoints: Attenuation: Loss of signal strength over distance
Measured in decibels (dB)
Causes: Resistance of medium, signal absorption
Effects: Signal becomes weak, errors increase, limited range
Solutions: Use amplifiers, repeaters, or regenerators

questions.question: Explain the Nyquist Theorem and Shannon's Capacity theorem. How do they differ?
keyPoints: Nyquist: Max data rate = 2B × log₂M (noiseless channel)
Shannon: Capacity = B × log₂(1+S/N) (noisy channel)
Nyquist considers signal levels (M)
Shannon considers signal-to-noise ratio
Nyquist sets upper bound, Shannon provides theoretical limit

questions.question: Compare guided and unguided transmission media with examples.
keyPoints: Guided: Physical path for signals (twisted pair, coaxial, fiber)
Unguided: Wireless transmission through air (microwave, satellite, radio)
Guided: More secure, less interference, limited range
Unguided: More flexible, susceptible to interference, wider coverage
Examples: Ethernet cable (guided), WiFi (unguided)

questions.question: Explain the different network topologies (Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh) with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Bus: Single cable, cost-effective, if cable fails network fails
Star: Central hub, easy to manage, hub failure affects all
Ring: Circular connection, predictable performance, failure affects network
Mesh: Multiple connections, highly reliable, expensive and complex
Choice depends on cost, reliability, and scalability needs

questions.question: What is encoding in data communications? Explain NRZ and Manchester encoding schemes.
keyPoints: Encoding: Converting digital data to digital signals
NRZ: Non-Return-to-Zero - constant voltage for 1, zero for 0
Manchester: Transition in middle of bit - 0→1 transition for 0, 1→0 for 1
Manchester provides clock synchronization but needs double bandwidth
NRZ simple but may lose synchronization in long transmissions

questions.question: Explain the concept of noise in data communications. Discuss various types of noise and their effects. Also explain how noise affects channel capacity.
keyPoints: Noise: Unwanted electrical signals interfering with data transmission
Types: Thermal (random electron motion), Impulse (sudden spikes), Crosstalk (neighboring signals), Intermodulation (signal mixing)
Effect: Causes errors, limits data rate, degrades signal quality
Shannon's formula: C = B × log₂(1+S/N)
Higher noise reduces S/N ratio, decreases capacity
Solution: Shielding, error correction codes, signal conditioning

questions.question: Discuss the transmission impairments that affect communication systems. Explain the relationship between bandwidth, data rate, and signal quality.
keyPoints: Impairments: Attenuation (signal loss), Noise (interference), Delay (latency)
Attenuation: Distance-dependent signal loss measured in dB
Noise: Thermal, impulse, crosstalk - degrades signal quality
Delay: Propagation + transmission + processing + queuing
Bandwidth: Frequency range (Hz) - determines capacity
Data rate: Actual bits transmitted per second
Nyquist: Higher bandwidth → higher max data rate
Shannon: Noise limits achievable data rate
Trade-off: Need adequate bandwidth and low noise for reliable high-speed communication

questions.question: Compare twisted pair, coaxial, and optical fiber cables in terms of construction, bandwidth, attenuation, interference resistance, and applications.
keyPoints: Twisted Pair: Pairs of copper wires twisted together, up to 1 Gbps, high attenuation, susceptible to EMI, used in telephone and LAN
Coaxial: Central conductor with shielding, up to 10 Gbps, lower attenuation than twisted pair, better EMI resistance, used in cable TV and LAN
Optical Fiber: Glass/plastic fibers with light signals, up to 100+ Gbps, very low attenuation (0.2 dB/km), immune to EMI, used in backbone networks and long-distance
Selection: Based on distance, speed, budget, and environment

questions.question: Explain the concepts of latency, throughput, and bandwidth. How are they related in determining network performance?
keyPoints: Bandwidth: Maximum data transfer capacity (bps) - like pipe width
Latency: Total time for data to travel from sender to receiver (ms)
Throughput: Actual data successfully transmitted per second
Latency components: Propagation, transmission, processing, queuing delay
Bandwidth-delay product: Important for high-speed long-distance links
Relationship: High bandwidth doesn't guarantee low latency
Good performance needs: High bandwidth + low latency
Example: Satellite has high bandwidth but high latency due to distance
